data.title: Saturn Oil And Gas: The Tariff Bomb Is Here, Who Cares? (Reiterating Strong Buy)
data.description:
Introduction

Saturn Oil & Gas Inc. (OTCQX:OILSF) (TSX:SOIL:CA) released Q4, and full year 2024, earnings in mid-March. It was largely an immaculate report—achieving or exceeding previously given guidance on production and revenues, that should have moved the stock higher. The stock price barely fluttered and has moved down since. This was due in our view, to the over-arching concerns about oil prices (CL1:COM) and tariffs, and not anything to do with the company or its assets-which are posted below as a reminder. These are just tough times in the oil patch.

Of course, there was that little ripple about missing their EPS target bigly—by $0.25, after several quarters of beating them nicely. This was likely attributable to FX hedging costs and lower oil prices. They made up for it with efficient operations and increased production, and Adjusted Funds Flow-AFF hit a record for the quarter of $124 mm.

There was also a net debt bump taken in the quarter for FX impacts due to the devaluation of the Looney. John Jeffrey, CEO, commented during the call:

Again, this just demonstrates the sensitivity of foreign exchange on our net debt and supports Saturn's decision to layer on that foreign exchange hedge to protect interest and principal. Again, adjusting for currency, our gross and net debts are exactly on track to achieve our guidance set out earlier in the year.

We have discussed previously there is a disconnect between the valuation of companies like Saturn and their 2P reserves potential. That could not be more apparent here. The slide below shows (CAD) valuations for the stock relative to variants of reserves. If we give them their 2P stock price of $13.77 CAD, at their current float of 185,600,000 shares, you get a capitalization of $2.55 billion CAD for Saturn.

Still using 4th grade arithmetic—as an aside, I want to give credit to my mom, who drilled me with flashcards in the evenings, for my absolute mastery of 4th grade arithmetic-times tables through 12, decimal point conversions, fractional conversions—I'm your guy. Thanks Mom, I should note that's about a 6.5X bump from Saturn's current valuation of ~$384 mm CAD. Of course, that reserves valuation, and no company is trading anywhere near to that in the current climate, but it is comforting nonetheless to have built in upside while waiting for a reset on oil prices.

So, Saturn is undervalued, but it is also delivering double-digit organic 2P growth as noted on the right side of the slide. 38% in 2024. Still using 4th grade arithmetic, that's almost a doubling every couple of years!!! Who else is delivering that kind of growth? Let's be real and acknowledge that at some point the Law of Large numbers will kick in, but until then... Katie, bar the door.

So, with that preface, I hope you will understand why earlier this week-maybe last week... I'd have to check, but who cares? I doubled my position in Saturn.

The thesis for Saturn

First, let's deal with tariffs. Our dependence on Canadian crude will in no way be lessened by the current, and we feel a short-lived tariff spat. There are a couple of reasons for this notion. Number one, all roads from the Western Canadian Sedimentary Basin—WCSB—run south, mostly save for the newly commissioned TMX line. That means that even if Canada wanted to sell their output to someone other than the U.S. they couldn't. Most Canadians are pretty cheesed off about this and are shaking fists, and rattling sabers at their elected leaders. But you know how that goes. Our favorite Canadian oil analyst, sage, and forecaster, Eric Nuttall, exemplifies the current outrage.

Energy imports are excluded on our end by the USMCA agreement. Oil prices from Canada will not go up as a result. This means there is no reason other than sheer market panic for the 7% decline in the shares today courtesy of the new tariff regime. Now on to Saturn.

With 83% of its production from long life, low decline oil and liquids rich gas dominated reservoirs, Saturn is a play on liquids. It's ability to grow organically, as noted above, gives them some optionality to bring future production forward when oil prices are higher. With 2P reserve life of 13.5 years, Saturn faces no drilling walls that would constrain revenues from lack of inventory-currently with 1,100 booked locations. The company has also made a strategic pickup in the quarter in the Brazeau area of the Cardium that facilitates new drilling with longer laterals on existing acreage. The Cardium is frack country and Saturn will allocate 21% of their capital budget to it in 2025.

The company's focus on return of capital fits nicely into my portfolio and is grounded in realistic product prices. Hedging for price volatility and FX impacts further buttresses the investment thesis for the company.

Review of Q4 2024 and 2025 guidance

Saturn turned in record Q4/24 production of 41,051 BOEPD, and 41,908 BOEPD exit production for the year. This bears the previous guidance range of 39,000 to 40,000 BOEPD. Adjusted funds flow for the quarter totaled $124 million-CAD, or $0.64 a share, a record for the quarter.

For the full year 2024, Saturn's AFF was $2.10 or $380 million-CAD, for which achievement, the company credited their ability to control costs, identify operational synergies and enhance margins. Net operating expense came to $19 BOE for the year and just over $18 BOE in the quarter, exceeding the $20 BOE target in previous guidance. Lower royalties and transportation expenses in both quarters and the year, contributed to an operating netback of just over $43 a BOE in both periods. This was in line with 2022 and 2023, a good performance over an inflationary period.

For this year, the slide below has the company's production and revenue guidance, which forecasts a 20% increase in AFF, and an increase in Free Funds Flow to a range of $125-$165 mm, dependent on oil prices. This is while maintaining production in the current range.

Source.

A catalyst for Saturn

Saturn booked 0.6 mm bbl of new reserves based on the OHML wells at Viewfield and had good success in transferring this concept to Spearfish. As noted in the call, this “unlocked” new barrels for development in an existing resource. John Jeffrey, CEO commented on this:

So, the exciting thing with the open hole Spearfish wells that Justin was just talking about is those weren't a conversion booking that is 100% additions to what we already had. So again, as we see $100 million of value, that is incremental to anything else we had.

In the success case for these 34 new wells to be drilled this year, we could see another ramp in booked reserves.

Risks

I don't think Saturn has any risks at this level that don't apply generally to the whole Canadian cohort—WTI prices—which are 55% hedged for 2025. FX impacts—which actually pump-up revenue as noted in the call, and tariffs—which make no sense and as noted are excluded from the U.S. tariff regime.

On the flip side, if the tariff thingie goes on too long, there is a mood among the electorate in Canada to impose export taxes on oil sent to the U.S. I don't think this is a big risk, but it's not zero. We take ~4 mm BOPD from the country, and it's up for question if other countries could, or would, provide oil cheaper.

Your takeaway

Saturn trades favorably in comparison with cohorts at 3.14X EV/EBITDA. Its Flowing barrel price works out to $20K per barrel, which given their netback of $43 per bbl provides a substantial cushion. Their 2P reserves cost is only about $4.00 CAD per barrel. To tie a bow on this notion, Saturn has a ROCE of between .60% and .75% depending on whose numbers you use. Either one puts them solidly in the buy column.

Analysts have them ranked in the Overweight column-which makes no sense to me, and suggests a lack of understanding on their part-this could be a tariff confusion spill over, but still. It's a little out of sync with their $2.75-$4.50 price targets as well. Just last August they were carrying a 5X multiple which had the stock at $2.12. That's a thirty percent bump from current prices and should be doable once this tariffs thingie clears up.

We like Saturn for the upside it presents when there is a reset in oil prices. It's price to reserves reveals a significant discount to its 2P valuation. A risk-tolerant investor should consider if a company like Saturn fits in their portfolio.

data.title: Andvari Associates Q1 2025 Letter
data.description:
Dear Friends,

For the first three months of 2025, Andvari is up 7.2% net of fees while the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) is down 4.3%.* Andvari clients, please refer to your reports for your specific performance and holdings. The table below shows Andvari’s composite performance while the chart shows the cumulative gains of a $100,000 investment.

This period of significant outperformance is welcome. Andvari has suffered—hopefully not for much longer—from a long bout of underperformance due to the absence of shares of the “Mag 7+” in client portfolios. The Mag 7+ list includes some of the largest and best performing companies in the S&P 500: Alphabet (GOOG,GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN), Apple (AAPL), Meta Platforms (META), Microsoft (MSFT), Nvidia (NVDA), Tesla (TSLA), and Netflix (NFLX). The chart below from WisdomTree illustrates the stark difference in performance between just the Mag 7+ and the remaining 492 companies in the S&P 500:

From the start of this year to April 1, the “S&P 492” was up 0.89% while the Mag 7+ component was down 13.12%.

With money flowing out of the Mag 7+ during the past quarter, the money seems to have flowed into the shares of many of the businesses we own. Although our holdings vary in size of revenues, enterprise value, and the products and services they sell, they do not vary in three attributes. Andvari believes our holdings to be (1) high quality businesses, (2) run by able managers, and (3) capable of allocating capital to produce above average returns over a long period of time. Below is our update on many of them.

ANDVARI HOLDINGS

Life Sciences and Companion Animals

Andvari has investments in four businesses that serve the life sciences and companion animal industries—companion animals are primarily the cats and dogs that are the typical pets for us humans. Mettler-Toledo (MTD) and Danaher (DHR) sell essential equipment and consumables into the life sciences and pharma industries. IDEXX (IDXX) and Zoetis (ZTS) serve the companion animal market with equipment, consumables, software, and pharmaceutical products, all of which makes the lives of veterinarians easier and improves the quantity and quality of the lives of animals.

All four companies are leaders in their respective markets and all benefit from solid growth tailwinds. Margins and returns are high, the businesses are not capital intensive, and revenue growth rates have generally ranged from mid- to high single digits.

However, as we’ve mentioned before in prior letters, due to the COVID era, a large amount of demand was pulled forward for these businesses. All four have been working through this overhang and their market valuations have declined as a result. Although it has taken longer than Andvari anticipated for growth to normalize for these businesses, when growth reverts to pre-COVID levels, we should see their share prices appreciate.

Nicotine and Tobacco

Last year, Andvari made its first investments in tobacco companies with the purchase of Philip Morris International (PM) and Altria (MO). At the time of our purchase, Philip Morris and Altria had underperformed the S&P 500 over the prior 5- and 10-year periods. Both traded at low valuations and with high dividend yields. But thanks to following the industry off and on for 10+ years, and thanks to many discussions with long-time shareholders of the companies, Andvari felt the time was right to make the plunge. The timing could not have been much better for us as both companies have so far contributed positively to Andvari’s recent overall performance.

The problem—or the feature, depending on your perspective—with the tobacco industry has been a declining population of cigarette smokers in developed countries. Over the last four or five years, the decline in these smoking populations has accelerated, which in part explains the poor share performance of the tobacco companies between 2017 and 2023. Despite this, the tobacco companies have maintained, or slowly increased, their revenues and profits with regular price increases on cigarettes.

More importantly, over the last two decades the constituents of the tobacco industry have poured money into research and development efforts to create reduced risk products to satisfy the desires of their traditional customers more safely. As a result, the industry players have come out with vastly safer products that deliver nicotine without the combustion process. Note well, it is the chemicals that come from the burning of tobacco that cause cancers, not nicotine itself. As a result, greater and greater numbers of smokers have been switching over to vaping, heat-not-burn products, and nicotine pouches.

The country of Sweden is one of the most extraordinary examples of an entire populace switching from cigarettes to nicotine pouches or their local variant called snus. As a result, smoking rates in Sweden fell from 40% of the population in 1976 to 5.8% in 2022. Cancer rates related to cigarette use plummeted and Sweden will soon be the first country in Europe where the smoking population will be less than 5% of its total.

Reduced risk products from the tobacco companies are undeniably good for public health. And these products have only started to prove they will be good for the tobacco companies that are transitioning their customers away from cigarettes. The nicotine pouch category in particular has been growing rapidly. You may have heard of the ZYN brand. Philip Morris is the brand owner after its purchase of Swedish Match in late 2022. ZYN has been growing between 40%-80% and this is despite an ongoing shortage of the product. Altria has a brand called on! that has been growing between 32%-48% over the last two years.

Finally, there are 1.1 billion nicotine users in the world. One billion of them are cigarette users and only one hundred million are users of reduced risk products. There is a long way to go for nicotine pouches and other safer products to take even half of the share of the entire nicotine market. As the new products grow their market share, they will have the same financial qualities of the cigarette business (extraordinary margins, high returns, and highly predictable revenues) which will benefit shareholders. Governments will benefit by a significant source of tax revenues not disappearing with the demise of cigarettes, and society will benefit from a dramatic decline in smoking-related cancers.

Rates, REITS, and Real Estate

Our holdings that have been most sensitive to interest rates remain well-poised for outperformance. Our two cell tower REITs—American Tower (AMT) and SBA Communications (SBAC) —have continued to reduce their debt and leverage ratios. Both have also recently increased their quarterly dividend payments to shareholders. American Tower is most notable in this regard as it paused dividend increases for a year.

CoStar Group (CSGP) continues their investments in the space of online residential property portals. They own fast-growing challenger brands in the U.S. and U.K. in the form of Homes.com and OnTheMarket, respectively. During the past quarter, CoStar also made a bid to acquire Domain Group, the owner of online portals for residential and commercial real estate listings in Australia. Andvari continues to root for CoStar’s success in the residential space, but we do not think success is necessary to achieve good shareholder returns going forward. We believe the market is slightly undervaluing CoStar’s commercial real estate business lines because heavy investments in the residential category have decreased overall margins. If CoStar can generate value from residential, that will be excellent. If not, the investments will end and company-wide margins will shoot up dramatically, and hopefully the share price as well.

TARIFF TIME

With tariffs dominating news headlines, we must first confess we cannot predict what exactly the impact will be upon our holdings. However, it is still worth discussing the degree to which tariffs may or may not affect the companies in which we are invested. For several reasons, our companies are prepared to manage through this new paradigm.

First, tariffs in most historical cases have been applied to physical goods and commodities. Because Andvari owns many businesses that sell intangibles like software or services, we are well positioned. Our software businesses include Constellation Software (OTCPK:CNSWF), Topicus.com (OTCPK:TOITF), Tyler Technologies (TYL), and Chapters Group (OTCPK:MDCKF). We also own many businesses we think of as primarily service-based, such as:

  • Mastercard (MA). The company enables the transfer of value using technology and provides other value added services to financial institutions and merchants.

  • Rollins (ROL). One of the largest pest control services in the U.S.

  • Arthur J. Gallagher (AJG). They provide insurance and reinsurance broking all around the world.

  • S&P Global (SPGI). Provides credit ratings and data services to businesses and financial institutions.

  • Kelly Partners (OTCQX:KPGHF). Provides accounting services to small and medium sized businesses primarily in Australia, but increasingly in the U.S. and U.K.

  • Booz Allen Hamilton (BAH). Provides management and technology consulting services primarily to the U.S. government. This is a small, new investment for Andvari that we started after the share price fell significantly due to negative news about DOGE and aggressive government efficiency efforts.

Again, it is unlikely that any of the above businesses will directly feel the effects of tariffs to the same extent as those who manufacture physical goods in lower-cost countries and then import them to the United States.

Second, for the handful of companies in Andvari portfolios that do make physical goods, they all share a mitigating factor: they all have above average pricing power. The source of their pricing power stems from selling products that are critical to the end user and yet are a small proportion of the customers’ total costs. They also often benefit from high switching costs. Danaher, Mettler-Toledo, Zoetis, and IDEXX fall into this camp, as well as TransDigm.

More specifically about Danaher, their customers design its equipment and consumables into pharma and biologic production lines. If a research lab has made the significant investment to standardize on Danaher equipment, it must continue to buy the appropriate consumables and receive regular maintenance services from Danaher. Thus, if a lab needs to grow, there is little chance a lab would stop buying from Danaher. If a pharmaceutical or biologic manufacturer needs to grow, there is virtually no chance they will switch to similar equipment unaffected by tariffs. It is just too costly and risky to be recertified by the FDA or its European equivalent, the EMA. Danaher can raise prices on its customers given the value provided by its products and the switching costs involved.

TransDigm (TDG) is a business with extreme pricing power. They make hundreds of different parts that go into commercial and military aircraft. Because TransDigm owns the intellectual property or is the sole source provider of nearly all its products, it can easily raise price increases when there is inflation or when its costs rise.

Andvari also owns two companies that are distributors of physical goods: O’Reilly Auto Parts (ORLY) and Pool Corporation (POOL). Out of all the businesses we own, these two might face the greatest risks of rising costs due to tariffs, but we shall see. O’Reilly is one of the largest distributors of auto parts in the United States and they do most of their business in this country. However, they do source many of their products from countries like China, Mexico, and India. Back during COVID and President Trump’s first tariffs, the company started diversifying it supplier base to create a more resilient supply chain and to minimize as best they could the effects of tariffs. But even with tariffs on products that they could only source from China (like suspension components), O’Reilly and its competitors were still able to pass on 20%-25% price increases directly to their customers.

Regarding Pool Corporation, they are the largest distributor of pool-related products and supplies in North America. We like that the company has never competed solely on price. They sell based on value and providing the best customer experience. They have also historically been able to pass on inflationary price increases to their customers.

ANDVARI TAKEAWAY

We own a collection of excellent and highly resilient businesses. Several were started over one hundred years ago and have survived wars, depressions, pandemics, and everything else in between. Philip Morris was founded in 1847; Standard & Poor’s was founded in 1860; Orkin, the core brand of Rollins’ pest service business, was founded in 1901; Booz Allen was founded in 1914; for Mettler-Toledo, the Toledo Computing Scale and Cash Register Company was founded in 1901 while Mettler Instruments AG was founded in 1945; Arthur J. Gallagher was founded in 1927; O’Reilly Auto Parts was founded in 1957. Although we cannot brag about the founding dates of our software businesses to the same extent, they also are highly durable businesses due to the mission critical products they sell into niche markets. Although the next year or two might be unpleasant for shareholders, our businesses will prove their resiliency yet again.

As always, I love to hear from clients and anyone else. Please contact me with your thoughts, comments, or questions.

Sincerely,

Douglas Ott

data.title: March Jobs Report: Robust Payroll Growth Despite Tariff Uncertainty
data.description:
Equity investors have had a lot on their minds this past week. With tariff news in focus, the monthly jobs release was unlikely to be at the top of the radar for most participants. Expectations heading into the release were managed appropriately, setting up a low bar for a positive surprise to end the trading week. For bruised investors, the morning report should have provided some boost to morale.

The Labor Department reported Friday that employers added a blowout 228,000 jobs in March. This compares to an unrevised total of 151,000 jobs in February and a revised total of 117,000 jobs following a 34,000 negative revision reported in this morning’s release. This negative revision was in addition to a downward revision to January’s data of 14,000.

Stocks were tumbling ahead of the release. Yesterday, the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI) was down over 1,000 points throughout the day. The blue-chip index ultimately closed about 1,700 points lower. The S&P 500 (SPY) also joined correction territory with the Nasdaq (NDX), following its 4% drop over the course of the trading day.

Stocks remained shell-shocked in the pre-market trading hours, with all three indexes deep in the red. Meanwhile in the bond market, 10-year Treasury’s dipped further below 4%, continuing its path downward.

The highlights in today’s job report clearly weren’t as eventful as the ongoing drama surrounding tariffs, but the report still provided valuable clues as to the foundational strength of the economy. Here’s what to know about the March jobs report.

What Sectors Added Jobs In March?

Job gains were widely disbursed across all sectors in March. Familiar sectors, such as healthcare, continued adding payrolls through the month. In March, the sector added 54,000 jobs, above the 52,000 average over the last 12 months.

The gains in healthcare were supplemented by 24,000 job additions in social assistance, with individual and family services adding 22,000 jobs during the month.

Retail trade benefitted from a return of striking workers with food and beverage retailers. The sector added 24,000 jobs overall, a notable change from the flatline level of growth seen in the prior month.

A standout sector was transportation and warehousing, which rose by 23,000 jobs in March, nearly double the average monthly gain of 12,000 over the last 12 months.

These gains were offset in part by losses in the federal workforce. During the month, the government shed another 4,000 jobs after losing 11,000 in February.

Aside from the declines in the federal workforce, the data continues to show that the labor market is robust and healthy.

March Unemployment And Wage Growth

The jobless rate has continued to track rangebound over the last several months, despite notable fluctuations in job growth. This theme continued into March, with unemployment up slightly to 4.2% from 4.1% in February. The increase is worth mentioning but not a concern, in my view.

The labor force participation rate also held steady in the 62% range. Wages, meanwhile, grew 0.3% during the month, in-line with expectations and at the same pace as in February. A higher rate of inflation in the months ahead could result in upward pressure on wages, though this is unlikely given the reduced bargaining power of the current workforce.

Those arguing the economy is entering a period of stagflation may see correlation in the months ahead if wages continue to rise in the face of weak job growth. However, this hasn’t materialized just yet, in my view.

Fed Policy Implications

Long-term inflation expectations have begun to increase primarily as a result of tariff dynamics. For example, the median expected inflation rate in three years, according to the New York Federal Reserve’s (“Fed”) inflation expectation chart is 3%. This is up from 2.7% in the same period last year.

Labor dynamics, on the other hand, haven’t necessarily factored in as significantly as the possible effects from tariffs. Today’s job release shouldn’t materially impact the existing dynamics. A slowdown in hiring, however, combined with rising inflation will validate growing concerns regarding stagflation. This would pose significant challenges for the Fed. At present, I don’t share these concerns, and looking ahead, I continue to see the Fed holding steady on rates through at least the first half of the year. This would be in-line with existing expectations projected by the CME’s FedWatch Tool.

Final Thoughts On The March Jobs Report

Equity investors needed a breath of fresh air following a brutal week on Wall Street, and the March jobs release offered a low floor for better-than-expected news to end the week. With just 125,000 jobs expected to be added during the month, odds were favorable for a surprise to the upside. The announcement of 228,000 jobs added for the month should provide some solace to market observers that are likely nursing heavy monthly losses in their portfolios.

The jobs data showed that the labor market is still on solid footing despite reductions in the federal workforce and rising uncertainty in the business environment as a result of vacillating developments on the tariff front. In an uncertain environment, one could reasonably reason that business leaders would tap the brakes on further investment, which would negatively impact hiring. Stellantis’ (STLA) recent decision to idle factories in Canada and Mexico in response to tariffs supports this presumption. Today’s labor data, however, shows that broader economic activity is still churning along, albeit with less gusto than in previous months.

Hiring hasn’t necessarily been negatively impacted on a material basis, though one can asterisk a “yet” at the end of that statement. From a Fed policy perspective, the data is in-line with recent commentary from Chairman Jerome Powell, and I do not expect today’s report to be the groundbreaking development necessary for a pivot on the trajectory of interest rates. The report rather should simply serve as a positive break of good news in an otherwise bruising week.

data.title: Goodyear Tire & Rubber Company's Surge Was Justified
data.description:
April 3rd ended up being a brutal and bloody day for the stock market. After President Donald Trump announced sweeping global tariffs, markets plunged. They were already down meaningfully this year. But during the day, the Dow Jones Industrial Average fell by 4%. The S&P 500 (SP500) dropped 4.8%. And of course, the worst performer was the Nasdaq (COMP:IND). It plummeted 6%.

However, not every company in the market fared poorly. One shocking development was that shares of The Goodyear Tire & Rubber Company (NASDAQ:GT) skyrocketed. At one point, they were up more than 15%. And by the end of the day, they closed up 11.7%.

As a shareholder of the company with a large chunk of my portfolio dedicated to it, I was obviously happy. The rest of my portfolio suffered during this time, as did the portfolio of probably every investor who wasn't short something. So it was nice to see this reprieve. Keep in mind that I have long been bullish about the company. Since my last article about the company where I reaffirmed it as a "strong buy" candidate back in February of this year, the stock has risen by 9%. That is significantly better than the 9.8% plunge experienced by the S&P 500.

At first glance, The Goodyear Tire & Rubber Company might seem like the kind of prospect that would experience a big hit. After all, the US imported about $17.04 billion worth of tires and tubes last year. Data is difficult to come by. However, in 2019, the US imported about 25.8% of the tires consumed that year.

There are a couple of reasons why the market responded favorably to The Goodyear Tire & Rubber Company during the day. First and foremost, the market is probably finally recognizing the value that management's actions have brought and will bring to the company and its investors. In addition to this, the company has a large manufacturing presence here at home that should help to insulate it from foreign competition during these uncertain times. This only reinforces my previous "strong buy" rating on the stock.

Reasons to be optimistic

Think anybody would disagree with me in saying that the past few years have not been particularly pleasant for The Goodyear Tire & Rubber Company and its investors. In the chart above, you can see the financial performance for the last three completed fiscal years. Revenue during this time dropped from $20.81 billion to $18.88 billion. 2023 ended up being a difficult year for the business. Profits and cash flows largely worsened. However, in 2024, the enterprise saw some improvements on its bottom line. Even though revenue still dropped, certain cash flow metrics and adjusted profit figures ended up being higher year over year.

A lot of this improvement can really be attributed to some restructuring initiatives that the company has undergone. In November 2023, management announced a transformation plan that they called Goodyear Forward. This came after some attention from activist investors who were pushing for change. In a previous article, which you can read here, I talked more about the specifics of that plan. However, as the image above illustrates, the basics involve selling off certain non-core assets and engaging in other restructuring activities to drastically improve margins.

In my previous article about the company, I detailed how the firm is expecting these changes to continue moving forward. In fact, management expects an additional benefit in terms of annual run rate savings of $750 million this year. This followed $500 million in annual run rate savings that the company achieved last year. These improvements will come from footprint and plant optimization initiatives, changes in how the company buys its products, cutting certain corporate expenses, optimizing its prices, and addressing supply chain and research and development issues. Eventually, the goal is to get savings of up to $1.50 billion on an annual run rate basis. Obviously, paying down debt from the $1.6 billion in asset sales the company has already either completed or announced should help in that regard.

This is all great, and I am a firm believer that shares of the company are drastically undervalued. Personally, I believe that a very conservative price for shares should be around $15. And depending on how things go with the economy and the company's cost-cutting initiatives, a price well in excess of that could be possible. Having said that, there has been a big overhang that has proven to be problematic for the company in recent years. This means that the total volume of tires sold by the company has been on the decline.

From 2022 to 2024, the company went from shipping 184.5 million tires a year to shipping 166.6 million. The one bright spot during this time has been its operations in the Asia Pacific region. Over the last three years, total unit sales grew from 34.4 million to 36.1 million. Outside of that region, however, the picture has been problematic. In the Americas, sales have fallen from 95 million units to 81.6 million. And in the EMEA (Europe, Middle East, and Africa) region, volumes fell from 55.1 million to 48.9 million.

The substantial decline in the Americas has been largely the result of increased competitiveness in the US when it comes to lower-priced tires and, in part, because of some weakness involving distribution changes in Latin America and economic weakness in Brazil. In the EMEA region, most of the decline for the company came from 2022 to 2023. That plunge of 5.2 million tires seems to have been driven by the same competitive pressures impacting the Americas and because of economic weakness in Turkey.

As you almost certainly know by now, President Trump made the decision to place tariffs on the world in a bid to bring back manufacturing jobs to the US and to try to negotiate what would be considered in his eyes to be a fairer trade balance between the US and the rest of the world. In another article, which you can read here, I provided my analysis of the impact those tariffs will likely have on the US economy and the stock market. In short, I believe that this move risks pushing the U.S. into a period of stagflation followed by a decline in GDP.

The market clearly perceived the tariffs as being a net positive for The Goodyear Tire & Rubber Company. This is because the company has said in the past, including even earlier this year, that low-priced imports are taking away its market share. Certainly, we can see when looking at the data that the imports of tires increased over the past several years. From 2017 through 2019, the value of tire imports to the U.S. rose from $12.16 billion to $13.21 billion. Because of the COVID-19 pandemic, there was a plunge to $11.92 billion in 2020. But after that, we saw a surge in the value of imports. By 2024, we had seen an increase to $17.04 billion. It is worth noting that, during this time, the value of higher exports from the U.S. actually dropped marginally from $3.40 billion in 2017 to $3.23 billion last year.

While we don't have year-by-year import data when it comes to the quantity of tires for that same window of time, we do have data for 2023 and 2024. Thailand was the largest importer of tires to the U.S. They imported 50.5 million units last year. That's up from the 42.5 million reported the year before that. Mexico saw an increase from 24.5 million to 25.1 million. Vietnam was in third place with an increase from 17.6 million to 20.7 million. As the chart above illustrates, the top ten largest sources of foreign tires into the U.S. last year ended up being responsible for 169.8 million tires. That's up from the 157.3 million a year earlier for a year-over-year gain of 7.9%.

Over the past few years, it seems as though these countries have eaten The Goodyear Tire & Rubber Company’s lunch when it comes to the U.S. I don't have evidence for this, but I would assume that the same holds true of the EMEA regions as well. As the aforementioned chart illustrated as well, though, Donald Trump has assigned tariffs of varying levels on those countries. Imports from Thailand, for instance, will have a 36% tax put on them. From Mexico, it's 25%. Vietnam and Cambodia are the highest at 46% and 49%, respectively. The lowest is Chile at only 10%.

This should bode well for The Goodyear Tire & Rubber Company. While the company did say in the past that it absolutely imports and exports tires, it seems as though it produces a large chunk of its tires that it sells in the U.S. here at home. Specific numbers aren't publicly available. But we can get some ideas based on the company’s manufacturing footprint. At the end of its 2024 fiscal year, it had 53 manufacturing facilities in operation globally. These are spread across at least 20 countries. In the Americas, it has 16 tire plants, four chemical plants, three tire manufacturing equipment plants, two tire retread plants, two aviation retread plants, and one mix plant. In fact, of its facilities that are dedicated solely to tire manufacturing, half of them are located here in the Americas. Eight of these tire plants are located specifically in the U.S.

This makes a great deal of sense to me when you consider how costly tires must be to ship. They're certainly not small and they're certainly not light. Producing them domestically can make a great deal of sense. However, this is not the only benefit that the company might have because of the tariff news. Over the last three years, The Goodyear Tire & Rubber Company has actually increased the number of tires that it produces for OEMs like vehicle manufacturers. That number has grown from 40.6 million units to 45.9 million. However, that still means that 120.7 million, or 72.4%, of the company’s tires last year were focused instead on the replacement market.

According to Cox Automotive, the recent tariffs that had been put in place on the automotive space will push the average price of a new vehicle that is imported into the U.S. up by $6,000. For those that are produced here, the increase will probably be around $3,000 since only 40% to 50% of all the content that goes into domestically produced cars is ultimately sourced from here at home. This price increase should very quickly impact the price of new vehicles that consumers are having to contend with. In all likelihood, it will lead to a rather substantial decline in the number of new vehicles sold in the US each year. One analyst from BofA Securities, John Murphy, estimated that we could see a reduction of about 3 million new vehicles being sold in the U.S. each year as a result of these tariffs. That is pretty significant when you consider that, in 2024, about 16 million new vehicles were sold here at home.

We have already started to see evidence that consumers are rushing to dealerships in order to pick up new cars while the getting is good. In March, for instance, the seasonally adjusted annual rate of new vehicle purchases was an astounding 17.8 million. That's 11.2% higher than what it was one year earlier. At some point, prices will rise, especially as dealer inventories drop. That will obviously hurt the sale of original equipment tires that The Goodyear Tire & Rubber Company sells. However, these changes should increase the demand for used vehicles as well. Those who would buy new vehicles will also decide to keep their existing vehicles longer as a result of these price hikes. So at the end of the day, there should be an increase in demand for the firm’s replacement vehicles. Obviously, the better competitive position that the company is being put in because of the tariffs should also prove bullish.

Unfortunately, the picture does become less clear at this stage. Because we are in uncharted territory, we don't know the full impact that these tariffs will have on the demand for tires as a whole. For instance, if we go into a recession, the number of tires demanded should decrease as individuals and companies drive less. This would certainly impair the demand for replacement tires. There's the prospect that driving could become even less common in the near term as cash-strapped consumers are faced with significant inflationary pressures. Tires are also not an inexpensive purchase.

As an example, last year, replacement tires cost anywhere between $80 and well over $1,000, depending on their size and other attributes. And unfortunately, 59% of Americans say that they cannot afford an emergency expense of $1,000 or more. I suspect that number will go up as well. This is just a small piece of the pie. We could attempt to speculate further from this point. But without concrete data, it might be nothing more than an exercise in futility.

Takeaway

At the end of the day, I am a value investor focused on the long-term outlook. The U.S. has seen dark days in the past, and we will see dark days in the future. It looks as though the foreseeable future will be filled with uncertainty at best and pain at worst. From what data we do know, it does seem as though The Goodyear Tire & Rubber Company offers investors an interesting opportunity. Add on top of the positive impact the company will likely have, at least in the near term, as a result of these tariffs, the fact that management continues to make improvements from a structural standpoint, and I firmly believe that the enterprise is worthy of the "strong buy" rating I have had it at recently.

data.title: Union Pacific Combines A Wide Moat, High-Margins, And A Great ROE
data.description:
Introduction

The Union Pacific Corporation (NYSE:UNP) is a major railroad holding company in the U.S. that is the parent of the historical Union Pacific Railroad. It plays a crucial role in the backbone of the U.S. transportation industry with its vast network of tracks across 23 states, linking all major gateways in North America. Through acquisitions, it grew to over 32,000 miles of track, transporting various goods such as agricultural products, chemicals, automobiles, etc.

And while the company's stock has not performed well over the past year, dropping 2.83%, UNP has a reputation of being a tremendous compounded with an incredible moat. That's why want to dwelve into the company to figure out if today's price is great while it stagnates, to invest in a crucial company that is well positioned for America's future.

Segment overview

Union Pacific separates its business into three main segments: Bulk, Industrial, and Premium.

Bulk includes all commodities shipments in large quantities, such as grain, fertilizers, and coal. It includes key routes such as the Central Corridor and the Overland Route. This segment generally contributes to 35-40% of freight revenue historically, though coal's decline as an energy source in America has hit freight revenues. In 2024, Bulk generated $7.2B in total sales, with the "bulk" of it coming from grain & grain products. A strong point of this segment is the use of base units that are dedicated to a single commodity, which are all the same for efficiency and therefore reduce costs.

The Industrial segment ships raw materials and manufactured goods that are crucial for various sectors such as construction and energy. It includes chemicals, metals, and energy products. It has key hubs in Houston, Salt Lake City, and Chicago; however, the segment relies on a mix of unit trains which makes the segment less efficient compared to bulk. It now generates the biggest portion of revenue in 2024, with $8.44B.

The premium segment focuses on high-value and time-sensitive freight. It has become UNP's fast-growing area, which has capitalized on current market trends such as e-commerce. It serves products such as intermodal and automotive. It employs the now-famous PSR system to optimize speed and reliability, which has become critical for time-sensitive cargo. UNP can leverage its vast network to directly become dominant in a sector, for example, UNP handles close to 50% of West Coast intermodal rail. It has key routes such as the Sunset Route and the I-5 corridor. It generated $7.16B in 2024, up from $6.97B in 2023.

Market Dynamics and Outlook

In a broad sense, economic growth increases freight demand and vice versa, which in today's tough economic backdrop can be hard to figure out. UNP has played a pivotal role in U.S. infrastructure, with a close duopoly in the West with BNSF that absolutely dominates western freight rail. Looking east, UNP has competition from CSX and Norfolk (NSC) on interline traffic at gateways and up north with Canadian National (CNI) and Canadian Pacific (CP) in cross-border trade.

If we directly compare to other modes of freight transport, trains have a significant cost advantage compared to air cargo or trucking, where most of the value comes from last-mile delivery. UNP's intermodal competes on cost with around $0.05/ton-mile vs. $0.15 for trucks, and truck driver shortages also tilt long-haul transport to rail.

For crude and other energy products, pipelines are the most efficient and cheapest way to move commodities, yet UNP's flexibility keeps the model relevant for spot demand. Barges also compete with rail with even lower costs, though the Mississippi River system is more prone to climate change and therefore unpredictability. Rivers can also fall short in terms of speed and reach.

UNP expects a strong performance in 2025 amid a mixed economic backdrop thanks to its industry-leading operating ratios, in part due to fewer but longer trains. It also plans on growing EPS with a 3-year CAGR target at high-single/low-double digit. Meanwhile, the company updated its Capital Plan to $3.4B, with the bulk going to infrastructure replacement.

Valuation

UNP has a valuation in line with the industry, posting a P/E of 21.39, 5% lower than the sector median. EV/EBITDA is also fairly high at 14.36, but what really intrigued me would be the company's P/B of 8.48 which is around 213% higher than the sector median. Now, the P/B ratio and the other ratios might be elevated due to investors recognizing Union Pacific's wide moat, but also its infrastructure is a wide range of irreplaceable assets that have replacement costs significantly higher than book value. Furthermore, UNP's book value has been heavily depreciated due to decades-old railways, further skewing the P/B ratio upward.

This takes me to the strength of UNP and its substantial profitability metrics, which deserved the coveted A+ grade from Seeking Alpha. UNP's Return-On-Equity stands at a staggering 42.6% thanks to its high net margin and its aggressive buybacks reducing equity. This, accompanied by its industry-leading ROA and ROTC, tells us how UNP is incredibly efficient and profitable.

This efficiency also allows the company to have higher margins than its peers, and I have to say that I did not expect railway companies to have such high net and LFCF margins.

Looking at capital return to shareholders, UNP has a plan of $4 to $4.5B in share repurchases in 2025, which is part of the new 100 million shares repurchase authorization. It would reduce shares outstanding by a further ~16.4%. Now, if we look back, UNP has already reduced its share count by a massive 28.85% since 2015, which makes the company an absolute cannibal. This is also accompanied by a cool 2.23% dividend yield that has been growing by an astounding 10.25% CAGR over the last 10 years. It is also relatively safe, with an A grade in dividend safety according to Seeking Alpha.

Risks

Union Pacific is dependent on fuel prices, if these go up, this would severely impact the operation, especially with the company's assumption of $2.5 per gallon of Diesel. Fuel prices also account for a substantial portion of its operating expenses, so if there is $1/gallon swing, UNP could add around $1-1.2B in costs (although most costs would be recovered via surcharges).

The use of longer trains boosts efficiency but could add bigger chances of delays and bottlenecks in case of a breakdown. Longer trains can also strain terminal capacity, increasing dwell times.

UNP is also operating in a competitive landscape, with strong competition coming from the deep-pocketed Berkshire Hathaway's BNSF that is augmenting CapEX and could erode UNP's intermodal edge.

Conclusion

I believe that Union Pacific is a great buy at this price, with such profitability metrics and its gigantic buyback program. The company has a great moat, allowing UNP to keep its high margins and its accretive return policy.

data.title: Apple Is Overpriced, Vulnerable And Heading Into A Storm
data.description:
When looking at articles published about Apple Inc. (NASDAQ:AAPL) in the recent past, we see several “Sell” or “Strong Sell” ratings, which is a rather unusual picture for Apple – an investor darling for a long time. While Wall Street is still rather bullish and have mostly rated Apple as a “Hold” or “Buy”, analysts here at Seeking Alpha are much more pessimistic. Almost three quarters of ratings are either “Hold” or “(Strong) Sell” ratings, with only a few authors being bullish.

I personally have been rather cautious about Apple as an investment, and rated the stock as a “Hold” in my last few articles. And since my last article in October 2024, the stock declined about 11% (and underperforming the S&P 500, which only declined 6.5%). However, previous “Hold” ratings in January 2023 and February 2024 might still seem too pessimistic with Apple generating double-digit returns for investors (however with the current volatility that might change quickly).

Several analysts were also turning rather bearish on Apple before Trump’s tariffs hit almost the entire world, with extremely high rates for major countries. Against the background of a quickly escalating trade war, we should take another closer look at Apple and answer the question if the stock is still a “Hold” or if the rating needs an upgrade. And considering the massive sell-off we saw yesterday – including Apple, which declined almost 10% - we start by looking at the valuation multiples.

Valuation Multiples

In the past, one of my major problems I saw with Apple was the high valuation multiples, making it not a good investment. Some might argue the correction in the last few weeks (and especially the crash yesterday) led to reasonable prices, and we should buy the dip. But Apple is still trading for 31.5 times earnings and 32 times free cash flow at the time of writing. And although Apple declined from its peak valuations of 39 times free cash flow and almost 43 times earnings, the stock is still expensive.

In my opinion, valuation multiples over 30 can only be justified when a business is able to report double-digit growth rates with a very high level of stability. And in the following sections, we will argue why I will put major question marks behind Apple’s potential to grow in the double digits in the foreseeable future.

First Quarter Results

In the first quarter of fiscal 2025, Apple did beat analysts’ expectations for earnings per share as well as revenue once again – for the sixth quarter in a row. When comparing the results to the same quarter last year, the results were not bad, but they were not great either.

Total net sales increased from $119,575 million in Q1/24 to $124,300 million in Q1/25 – resulting in 4.0% year-over-year top-line growth. Operating income increased from $40,373 million in the same quarter last year to $42,832 million in this quarter – resulting in 6.1% year-over-year growth. And finally, diluted earnings per share increased 10.1% year-over-year from $2.18 in Q1/24 to $2.40 in Q1/25. Earnings per share growing in the double digits was mostly a result of Apple’s share buybacks (we will get to this).

And while the income statement reported growth for the top and bottom line, free cash flow declined 28.0% year-over-year from $37,503 million in Q1/24 to $26,995 million in Q1/25.

Looking at the first quarter results of fiscal 2025, a valuation multiple over 30 is certainly not justified and when looking at revenue growth rates in the last decade, Apple is rather struggling to grow its top line – aside from a few selected quarters with high-growth rates (for example following the pandemic).

And while Apple grew its revenue with a CAGR of 7.90% in the last ten years, revenue growth was only 2.25% on average in the last three years. When looking at earnings per share, the picture is similar: In the last ten years, EPS grew with a CAGR of 14.19%, but in the last three years, the bottom line increased only with a CAGR of 2.72%.

Growth Is Not Enough

When looking at the results and growth rates in the last few years for Apple, it is extremely difficult to justify the current valuation multiples and the current stock price. However, when looking at analysts’ assumptions and growth expectations, we see high-growth rates. Between fiscal 2024 and fiscal 2024, analysts are expecting earnings per share to grow with a CAGR of 17.48%.

In my opinion, these assumptions are way too optimistic, and I don’t really see how Apple could grow 17% annually for the next few years. As always, growth can stem from many different sources – top-line growth, improving margins or buying back shares.

Growth from Share Buybacks

I already mentioned above, that share buybacks had a huge positive impact on bottom-line growth in Q1/25. Over the last twelve months, Apple bought 426 million shares back (about 2.75% of its total shares outstanding). And since Apple started buying back shares about 11 to 12 years ago, it decreased the number of shares outstanding from 26.55 billion to 15.15 billion – resulting in a CAGR of 4.5%.

And buying back about 4.5% of total shares outstanding annually certainly has a huge impact on the bottom line. However, the rather high valuation multiples Apple is trading for right now, make share buybacks less efficient. Nevertheless, Apple can continue to use its free cash flow for share buybacks and therefore contributes about 3% to bottom-line growth (even with current high valuation multiples).

Improving Margins

Aside from share buybacks, improving margins also contributed to bottom-line growth for Apple in the last few years. In the last few years, Apple improved its gross margin and operating margin, and this contributed to bottom-line growth.

It is always difficult to estimate how many more margins of a company can improve, but especially with increasing services sales and services taking a bigger and bigger part of total revenue, margins might also improve as the services business is a capital-light business – compared to selling hardware.

Top-line growth

However, one of the most important ways to grow for any business is by growing its top line. But we already saw above that top-line growth struggled in the last few years and Apple grew its revenue only in the low single digits and sometimes even had to report declining sales in some quarters.

Right now, Apple seems to be optimistic that Apple Intelligence might drive iPhone sales in the coming quarters. During the last earnings call, management gave an update on Apple Intelligence:

In October, we released the first set of Apple Intelligence features in U.S. English for iPhone, iPad, and Mac, and we rolled out more features and expanded to more countries in December. Now users can discover the benefits of these new features in the things they do every day. They can use writing tools to help find just the right words, create fun and unique images with Image Playground and Genmoji, handle daily tasks and seek out information with a more natural and conversational Siri, create movies of their memories with a simple prompt, and touch up their photos with clean up.

We introduced visual intelligence with camera control to help users instantly learn about their surroundings. Users can also seamlessly access chat GPT across iOS, iPadOS and MacOS. And we were excited to recently begin our international expansion with Apple Intelligence now available in Australia, Canada, New Zealand, South Africa, and the U.K. We're working hard to take Apple Intelligence even further. In April, we're bringing Apple Intelligence to more languages, including French, German, Italian, Portuguese, Spanish, Japanese, Korean, and simplified Chinese, as well as localized English to Singapore and India.

But I don’t know if Apple Intelligence is enough to drive sales for Apple’s hardware much higher. And when using a quick discount cash flow calculation with my usual assumptions (10% discount rate, last reported number of 15,151 million diluted shares outstanding and TTM free cash flow of $98.3 billion), Apple has to grow between 13% and 14% in the next ten years followed by 4% growth till perpetuity to be fairly valued right now (stock price slightly above $200). In my opinion, such growth rates are not reasonable for Apple – especially with the elephant in the room.

Tariffs, tariffs, tariffs

On his so-called “Liberation Day” Trump announced massive tariffs for almost every country around the world. Apple is especially hit hard due to its manufacturing presence in China and as Trump is considering China to be among the “worst offenders” the tariff rate for China is set to 54% (including earlier tariffs).

In the case of Apple, analysts are expecting the tariffs to have a negative impact of 500 basis points on gross margins and earnings per share, which might be about $1.20 lower. And this is already assuming that Apple is managing the disruptions quite well. If Apple doesn't raise prices for its products (which will lead to lower sales again), other analysts are expecting margins to drop even more. Seeking Alpha is writing in a news release:

Meanwhile, TF International Securities' Analyst Ming-Chi Kuo said if Apple keeps prices unchanged, its overall gross margin could significantly drop by an estimated 8.5% to 9%. He added that India and Vietnam are far more likely than China to secure U.S. tariff exemptions. The timeline is unclear but this would speed up Apple’s shift of assembly orders away from China until non-Chinese manufacturing can satisfy most U.S. demand.

And here we see one of the major problems, I have discussed briefly in some articles. The global economy is a complex, non-linear system with billions of connections and feedback loops. If anyone might think – and we are not naming names – that just slapping tariffs on almost every country around the world won’t have consequences, might face a harsh reality check in the coming quarters. In the case of Apple, it might lead to moving production away from China to other countries – but probably not the United States.

One doesn’t have to be a genius to see another consequence coming – other countries will retaliate, and Apple might get hit again. In the case of China or the European Union, it might be possible that Apple will get hit by tariffs on iPhones, iPads or the MacBook once again and will lead to higher prices for these products, which will probably lead to lower sales. On the other hand, the alternatives for the iPhone are limited, making it rather risky for Europe as well as China to impose high tariffs on these products.

And out of $124.3 billion in total sales, about 15% ($18.5 billion) are generated in Greater China and about 27% ($33.9 billion) are generated in Europe. Or to put it different: Only 42% of Apple’s sales are generated in the “Americas” region, which includes countries like Canada that have already been hit by tariffs from Trump in the recent past.

The Bigger Picture

Aside from Apple being hit by tariffs, which could lead to lower sales (due to potential higher prices for the products) and higher expenses (due to the tariffs already announced by Trump), there is a bigger picture we should also not ignore: The risk of the United States – and several other countries around the world – entering a recession increased dramatically.

And while the tariffs might already seem like a major problem for companies (including Apple), a recession in the United States and many European countries might create even bigger problems. With goods getting more expensive and people losing their jobs and, as consequence, having lower disposable income, the decision to buy a new iPhone or a new iPad will most likely be postponed.

The high levels of uncertainty will also have a negative impact on investments. Trump is expecting the tariffs to generate massive investments – and he is talking about $6 trillion in 2025 – the reality might be that investments will even be lower as most businesses will not invest in unstable times. Businesses from abroad might not invest at all, with the escalating trade war being a huge liability.

Finally, the stock market will also take a hit – and this first sell-off might only be the beginning and the first minor wave. As James A. Kostohryz points out in this article, the last 20 years of globalization have been extremely profitable for S&P 500 companies – and at least the stock market knows this. Combined with extremely high valuation multiples the stocks are trading for, the downside risk is rather high at this point.

Ghost of the Past

As long as we are talking about tariffs and recessions or depressions, we should also mention the Smooth-Hawley Tariff Act in 1930. And I certainly don’t want to claim that the Smooth-Hawley Tariff Act triggered the gigantic sell-off in U.S. stocks in 1930 and 1931, and it was certainly not solely responsible for the Great Depression, most scientists seem to agree that the Smooth-Hawley Tariff Act was not helpful and made the Great Depression worse.

And we should also not ignore that the world is much more interconnected due to the globalization processes in the last few decades, and the global trade relationships today can’t be compared to the trade relationships in the 1930s. Today, an escalating trade war probably has a much bigger negative effect than the Smooth-Hawley Tariff Act in 1930.

Conclusion

I have expressed my cautious view about the U.S. stock market for several quarters now, and I can live with the criticism of being too early and that I stopped dancing before the music stopped playing. And while we can argue that Trump’s politics might have triggered a sell-off, the tariffs are not the main reason for concern. The combination of the U.S. stock market trading for extremely high valuation multiples and rising delinquency rates, as well as the major risks banks have in the CRE portfolios.

In case of Apple, the company is faced by a similar cocktail of bad news: the stock is also trading for very high valuation multiples (making huge setbacks likely), tariffs will have a huge negative impact on Apple’s business and the company is selling products that belong in the category of consumer discretionary items and customers will probably postpone purchases in challenging times.

While I still think Apple is a great business and the company has a moat for part of its business, I would rate the stock as a “Sell” for the first time – especially for investors with a time horizon of only a few years.

data.title: April 2025 Outlook: Calamos Evolving World Growth Fund
data.description:
By Nick Niziolek, CFA, Dennis Cogan, CFA, Paul Ryndak, CFA and Kyle Ruge, CFA

During the first quarter of 2025, global markets sharply and quickly reflected the possible commencement of an inflection in investor sentiment to favor ex-US markets over the long-preferred US market. After years of US and foreign investors building up exposure to the US market, tariff and fiscal policy developments may be catalysts for capital migration out of the US and into ex-US markets at the margin.

US policies - from both DOGE and tariff perspectives - have introduced sentiment uncertainty into the US economic outlook, potentially indicating a crack in the US-exceptionalism trade. In contrast, in Europe, policymakers have initiated efforts toward increased defense and fiscal spending impulses, in concert with a stronger focus on innovation investment throughout the continent. Additionally, we continue to see increased policy efforts toward consumer support in China and a refreshed willingness from leadership to support the country’s most innovative companies. As fiscal spending shifts from the US to abroad, we believe investors are becoming increasingly excited about the potential for a pick-up in growth outside the US. (For more, see our recent post, “Overseas Investment Opportunities: US Policy Shifts Awaken the Sleeping Giants.”)

We welcome this environment. Our time-tested investment process is designed to quickly identify companies across an expansive universe that can benefit most from inflections. We then promptly size up our portfolio exposures to these opportunities. The fund remains diversified across a range of cyclical and secular themes, and we are excited that the breadth of opportunities has expanded across the global market, as previously out-of-favor areas of the markets enjoy renewed interest.

In this commentary, we examine some of these dynamics in more detail and provide insight into some of the ways we have positioned the fund to benefit from these changing dynamics.

China. One of the core principles of our investment philosophy is that capital will naturally move to where it is treated best. Throughout the years, we have frequently discussed the importance of economic freedoms in allocating capital globally. We believe economic freedoms—such as private property rights and fair and transparent capital markets—are as good as any scorecard at measuring the attractiveness of a destination for capital.

Capital has also quickly returned to markets where positive developments are occurring. Thus, from an investment standpoint, we believe the “delta” of a country’s economic freedoms (i.e., the degree of change that a country makes toward being more—or less—economically free) is more consequential than the absolute level of economic freedom. Through this framework, we can more fully appreciate the underperformance of Chinese equities over the past five years. More importantly, we can also appreciate the drivers of the recovery unfolding in Chinese equity markets.

Over the past decade, capital has steadily moved away from China as regulators cracked down on the private sector, increased the uncertainty about potential new regulations on industry, and extended restrictions on the movement of people and capital during the Covid crisis. Lately, however, we’ve seen some important indications of change—the all-important delta. For example, in recent months, President Xi hosted a roundtable with the same technology leaders he was cracking down on several years ago. We’ve also seen an increase in monetary and fiscal stimulus to support Chinese economic recovery, and the government communicating the importance of and its support for capital markets. Although much damage has already occurred, and economic freedoms in China are likely perceived as being much lower today than they were a decade ago, it does appear that an inflection point has been reached, with conditions now trending more positively than they have for many years.

That said, we continue to take a selective approach to investing in China. Where possible, we utilize structures that limit downside risk and focus on quality companies most exposed to the positive inflections we have identified. In recent months, we’ve seen a stabilization and some improvement in the Chinese property market, which remains a critical industry for both the psyche of the Chinese consumer and the economic growth it can provide.

Indeed, we believe we are beginning to see green shoots of improving economic activity in China following a loosening of fiscal and monetary policy. As the economy potentially faces new shocks from evolving global trade policies, we expect this support will increase as China seeks to create a “floor” under its economic growth forecasts of 5% in 2025.

US dollar. The move in the US dollar has historically been a telling indicator of the relative performance prospects of international versus US stocks. An extended period of a sideways-to-weaker dollar has historically supported higher relative returns for international stocks, as it traditionally encourages global allocation and capital flows into ex-US developed and emerging markets.

During the first quarter, softer US economic data, tariff concerns, and fiscal uncertainty contrasted strikingly with major international economies enacting economic stimulus policies. Against this backdrop, the US dollar began a steady decline lower from a starting valuation position, arguably at its most extended position over the past 50+ years (see figure below).

The US dollar Recently Traded over Two Standard Deviations above the Average of Other Currencies

We may not be witnessing a “weak dollar” but, for the first time in a very long time, a “strengthening euro.” Regardless, the change contributed to decisive international equity markets’ outperformance during the quarter. (For more, see our post, “Why the Dollar Inflection Should Not Be Ignored.”)

Major global assets tend not to move in a straight line in one direction. That said, we believe the dollar’s trajectory during the first quarter could be a preview of a sustained tendency for a sideways-to-downward US dollar.

Defense. We believe global defense spending is undergoing a significant shift as the US tightens its defense budget while Europe’s NATO members announce massive plans to ramp up spending on defense and security. In addition, with the change in US policy on Ukraine, European nations feel more intense pressure to accelerate and increase already planned spending.

This changing landscape has created new investment ideas supported by attractive fundamentals and valuations. (It’s a theme we’ve discussed throughout the past few years, including most recently in Jay Stewart’s post, “In Defense of Higher Spending: Geopolitics Creates Secular Opportunities.”)

Although it is unlikely European countries will reach the 5% of GDP target recently suggested by President Trump, 3% seems reasonable for many nations. Germany, in particular, is leading the way with aggressive spending ambitions while Estonia and Lativia have announced defense spending targets of 5% of GDP.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: TTEC Holdings: An Opportunity For Arbitrage Specialists
data.description:
Investment thesis

The share price of TTEC Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:TTEC) jumped 52.60% on April 2, after the board of directors announced it would “engage” with Founder, Chairman, and CEO Kenneth Tuchman on his $6.85 per share proposal.

According to a company news release on April 1, a special committee of the board advised investors it would review and evaluate the unsolicited offer from Tuchman. His offer was for all TTEC shares not owned by himself or his controlled affiliates.

What are shareholders and other investors to think of the proposal and the board’s response? In this article, I review the pros and cons of various actions.

I don’t think anyone other than arbitrage specialists should buy now. On the sell side, there is a lot of uncertainty and complexity to consider.

I rate the company a Hold.

About TTEC

The company calls itself a global customer experience (CX) outsourcing partner for “marquee and high-growth brands and public sector clients.”

In its 10-K for 2024, TTEC reported that it “designs, builds, and operates technology-enabled customer experiences across live interaction channels and provides data-driven digital solutions.”

Why? To help clients “improve customer satisfaction and loyalty, increase customer revenue and profitability, and optimize overall cost to serve.”

TTEC operates through two segments: TTEC Digital and TTEC Engage. The following slide from its February 2025 Investor Overview displays what the segments do and contribute to the parent:

The company had been based in Englewood, Colorado until late 2024, when it moved to Austin, Texas. In the Q4-2024 earnings release, management said Texas had been an important part of its business for decades, and the move would put it in a business-friendly environment and more. They added the relocation would not affect their other 70+ global locations.

Why is TTEC in play?

On September 30, 2024, the board issued a news release reporting it had received “an unsolicited, preliminary, non-binding proposal letter” from Tuchman. Tuchman, who owned about 58% of the company’s common stock at that time, planned to take TTEC private. He was offering $6.85 per share, which would have been a 69.14% premium on the Friday, September 27, closing price of $4.05.

Not surprisingly, the share price jumped to $5.87 by the close on September 30. But that turned out to be a high, as the price gradually drifted down to $3.27 by April 1, 2025. As it turned out, that was just the latest decline in a slump that began in August 2021:

That long slide has lasted over three and a half years now, which tells us TTEC is an unhealthy company. To look into the issues, let’s start with a 10-year chart of revenue, net income, and basic EPS:

The company’s net income and EPS peaked in the second quarter of 2021, just before the price started its long slide. Obviously, that’s no coincidence.

And then there is that more recent, and dramatic, plunge in net income and basic EPS, which occurred in the second quarter of 2024.

In the Q2-2024 earnings report, CEO Tuchman reported, “Our results this quarter are impacted by non-cash goodwill impairment charges and related tax adjustments. We continue to operate in a dynamic macroeconomic environment where clients are facing softer demand and budget constraints putting pressure on our top line in the Engage business, while our Digital business had a solid quarter.”

Tuchman also noted into the Q4-2024 earnings report, “With our strengthened leadership alongside the actions we have taken in the second half of the year, we are on our way to healthier financial performance in 2025 and beyond.”

He did not elaborate, but it would take a significant initiative or leadership to pull the company out of this swoon that has endured for 14 consecutive quarters now.

What’s the 2025 forecast?

Its full-year 2025 outlook, posted in the Q4 earnings report, calls for revenue of $2.014 billion to $2.064 billion, which would be less than 2024’s $2.208 billion. Adjusted EBITDA is forecast to be between $215 million and $235 million, compared with 2024’s adjusted EBITDA of $202.3 million.

So, expect slightly lower revenue and slightly higher adjusted EBITDA (and likely net income) in 2025. As for EPS, the Wall Street analysts have these estimates:

These estimates use normalized EPS, which looks far healthier than basic EPS. The former was a loss of $0.21 in 2024, while the latter was a loss of $6.74.

Nevertheless, Seeking Alpha always uses normalized EPS, so that is what we will compare here. Specifically, the Wall Street analysts expect normalized EPS to flip from a loss of $0.21 to a profit of $1.06. That seems an extreme rebound, and out of sync with the company’s modest expectations for adjusted EBITDA.

The same analysts have three Up revisions and three Down revisions, which don’t align with a burst of adjusted EBITDA.

Therefore, I’m going to ignore these estimates and suggest that EPS may pick up a little, but probably not enough to become profitable.

How should TTEC shareholders react?

There may not be any happy route for existing shareholders, particularly those who bought at a higher price during sunnier days for the company.

There are two possibilities going forward. First, the board of directors accepts Tuchman’s offer, and all existing shareholders are bought out (Tuchman already owns 58% of the firm’s shares). Second, the board rejects the offer and TTEC continues to operate as it has in the past. Let’s look at each of these in more detail.

Accepting the buyout offer: This would turn TTEC into a private company and seems most likely. Members of the board, directors, likely have a reasonably close affiliation with Tuchman already. While he may not have been involved in their hiring, they have undoubtedly gotten to know each other well while working on TTEC business.

Well-intentioned directors should have minority shareholders in mind but also should consider employees and other stakeholders. Since the firm’s fundamentals have kept sliding for over three years, they may consider a radical change, such as going private, most appropriate.

By privatizing, the company will save on the many compliance and reporting requirements for public companies. In turn, that should lead to lower SG&A costs and higher net income/EPS. It would also allow management more time to turn the firm around.

Rejecting the offer: The bad news for many shareholders would be capital losses. Over the past 52 weeks, the highest price was $9.50, and over the past decade, the high was $105.16. I suspect there are still some shareholders who paid more than $6.85 for their shares, and they will face losses.

On the other hand, shareholders who took positions for less than $6.85 would find an exit that gives them an easy way out and a capital gain that depends on how much they paid to buy their stock.

Rejection also would mean sticking with a company that hasn’t been able to reverse its slumping fortunes for more than three years. As noted, the CEO sounded optimistic notes in the most recent earnings report and the Wall Street estimate for this year is rosy, but we’ve seen many, many quarters where recovery efforts have not taken hold.

As I write this analysis on April 3, 2025, the markets are trying to figure out who will be affected by newly imposed tariffs, counter-tariffs, and by how much. TTEC, which is a consulting company, is unlikely to take any direct hits. However, some of its clients may be affected and cut back on customer experience spending.

Risk arbitrage

There may be an opportunity here for arbitrage specialists. If the board accepts Tuchman’s offer, all shares will immediately be worth $6.85. The April 3 closing price was $4.45, down $0.54 or 10.82%; the magnitude of the loss was probably due to the market-wide dip that came in reaction to the new tariffs.

The difference between $4.45 and $6.85 is $2.40 or 53.93%. Why not buy now or at a similar price and collect the difference?

First, the takeover offer may not be accepted, and you would end up owning a stock that has been declining in value for more than three years.

Second, if the offer is accepted, you still would not know how long it will take to finalize, so the actual return is unknown.

Third, if accepted, your capital is tied up until the takeover is complete, and you might have earned more in some other investment than in this one.

Summing up, for anyone other than an arbitrage specialist, this would be a speculative investment with known and unknown risks.

Other thoughts on TTEC

I’m curious. The Founder, Chairman, and CEO is offering to buy all shares he and his associates do not own, at $6.85 per share. So he must see a future for the company, but has he shared that with the rest of management?
