I would think that if he has a plan to transform TTEC, then that also would work for the public company. Public listing and compliance costs might make a difference to the bottom line, but I don’t see them being transformational.

On another topic, I wonder what institutional investors are thinking. They own roughly 38% of the shares not owned by Tuchman and associates, making them major partners. In the latest report, they bought and sold a roughly equal number of shares, which gives us no indication of their preferences.

Collectively, these investors own 18.25 million shares worth $81.21 million. The largest institutional holding is that of BlackRock, Inc. (BLK), which owns 1.775 million shares, worth $7.813 million.

Institutions tend to move very large blocks of shares and if they start to move one way or the other, then retail investors may be able to pick up a few clues. However, public and other non-insiders own just 1.33 million shares, so don’t expect the institutions to do much buying—there simply isn’t a suitably-sized inventory available.

TTEC Stock Valuation

I think the odds favor the board accepting Tuchman’s offer are greater than 50%, so I expect the share price will rise to $6.85, but have no idea when.

If the board rejects his offer, then there is a reasonable possibility the fundamentals will continue to slide and the price will keep falling. In that case, it’s possible it may be taken over by another firm with a plan, which would push the price up.

Given the current uncertainty, I rate TTEC a Hold.

Conclusion

Shares of TTEC shot up in the wake of news about CEO Tuchman’s offer to privatize the company. That may be good or bad news, depending on what price shareholders paid.

At the moment, I would argue it is a Buy only for arbitrage specialists because there is too much uncertainty for other investors. Those who are interested might watch and follow what institutional investors are doing.

data.title: Collect +7% From Energy Tollbooths
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

There are some businesses that demand significant upfront investment and years of development, but once in place, they become indispensable and generate steady, long-term cash flow. Their revenues are predictable and resilient, with only modest upkeep costs, allowing them to expand operations while distributing substantial profits to shareholders. One such industry is midstream energy, which serves as the critical backbone of the economy—operating the pipelines and infrastructure that keep energy moving.

It is hard to dislike the midstream business. It takes years to build (and costs several billions of dollars) and scale infrastructure, but once the network is established, it becomes vital for the country’s energy security. Companies establish long-term fee-based contracts that often insulate their earnings from commodity price swings. They are also structurally designed to distribute the bulk of their profits to shareholders. Investing in midstream is like having a tollbooth on the most important road in the city; tolls rise rapidly with inflation, and it doesn’t result in a decrease in traffic. This is the kind of business model we like to own at High Dividend Opportunities because this is the kind of investment that enriches our income stream with limited day-to-day drama. Without further ado, let’s dive into our top picks from this rewarding sector.

Pick #1: ET – Yield 6.9%

Energy Transfer LP (ET) is a midstream Master Limited Partnership that owns and operates one of the largest and most diversified portfolios of energy assets in the United States, with over 130,000 miles of pipeline and associated energy infrastructure. Energy Transfer’s strategic network spans 44 states, with assets in all of the major U.S. production basins and export terminals. Over 80 countries and territories receive exports from ET’s operations. Source

Note: ET is a Master Limited Partnership that issues a Schedule K-1 to unit holders

In addition to the direct ownership of midstream assets, ET also owns:

  • The general partner interests, the incentive distribution rights, and approximately 21% of the outstanding common units of Sunoco LP (SUN)

Sunoco is primarily engaged in energy infrastructure and the distribution of motor fuels in over 40 U.S. states, Puerto Rico, Europe, and Mexico. The company’s midstream operations include an extensive network of over 14,000 miles of pipeline and over 100 terminals, and its fuel distribution serves ~7,400 Sunoco and partner-branded locations and other commercial customers. In addition, Sunoco is the exclusive wholesale supplier of branded motor fuels, supplying to an extensive network of ~5,619 company and third-party-operated locations across the United States and Puerto Rico.

  • Lake Charles LNG Company

ET owns an LNG import terminal and regasification facility located on Louisiana’s Gulf Coast near Lake Charles, Louisiana. The import terminal has approximately 9.0 Bcf of above-ground storage capacity, and the regasification facility has a send-out capacity of 1.8 Bcf/d. Lake Charles LNG derives all of its revenue from a series of long-term contracts with a wholly owned subsidiary of Royal Dutch Shell.

  • The general partner interests and approximately 39% of the outstanding common units of USA Compression Partners LP (USAC)

USAC provides compression services to its customers, including both allowing for the processing and transportation of natural gas through the domestic pipeline system and enhancing crude oil production through artificial lift processes. These compression services play a critical role in the production, processing, and transportation of natural gas and crude oil. As of December 2024, USAC had 3.9 million horsepower in its fleet, and the company provides compression services to its customers under fixed-fee contracts with initial contract terms typically between six months to five years, where customers are required to pay a monthly fee even during periods of limited or disrupted throughput. This enhances the stability and predictability of its cash flows. Customers are mostly billed in advance of the service date, and there are annual inflation adjustments in these contracts.

ET fully and partially (through JVs) owns several other assets across the country, and describing them all is beyond the scope of today’s article.

Financials

During FY 2024, ET reported a 13% YoY growth in Adj, EBITDA, with all segments reporting strong growth, and Sunoco’s contribution stands with a 51% YoY increase due to elevated energy prices.

ET distributed $4.6 billion to unit holders in FY 2024, while generating $15.5 billion in adj. EBITDA and spending $6.1 billion towards growth and maintenance capital expenditures. The company’s $0.325/unit quarterly distribution calculates to a 6.8% yield.

ET is a More Mature Company Today

Following the pandemic-driven oil crash, ET has made several investments and acquisitions, making it a healthier and more mature company than ever before. Some of the MLP’s notable transactions include:

  • Enable Midstream Partners (December 2021): ET acquired Enable Midstream Partners for approximately $7.2 billion, adding significant natural gas gathering and processing assets to its portfolio.

  • Spindletop (March 2022): ET completed a $325 million bolt-on acquisition of Spindletop underground storage assets and ethylene storage header system.

  • Lotus Midstream (March 2023): ET purchased Lotus Midstream for around $1.4 billion, enhancing its crude oil transportation capabilities.

  • Crestwood Equity Partners LP (August 2023): This $7.1 billion acquisition expanded ET's gathering, processing, and storage operations in the natural gas, crude oil, and natural gas liquids sectors.

  • WTG Midstream Holdings LLC (July 2024): ET acquired WTG Midstream for approximately $3.25 billion, adding 6,000 miles of gas gathering pipelines and eight processing plants in the Permian Basin.

All these transactions were immediately accretive to the company’s distributable cash flows. ET’s DCF of $8.4 billion for 2024 provided a 1.8x coverage for its annual distribution. As a result, ET’s adj EBITDA experienced sustained growth, and its distribution enjoyed an impressive 21% CAGR over the past four years.

ET expects to spend $5 billion on growth capex in 2025, and projects adj. EBITDA between $16.1 and $16.5 billion (a 5.2% YoY increase at the midpoint). The company announced a long-term agreement with Cloudburst Data Centers to provide natural gas to its AI-focused data center development in Central Texas. Last month, ET placed the first 8 planned 10MW natural-gas-fired electric generation facilities into service. The company also entered a 20-year LNG Sales and Purchase Agreement with Chevron to supply 2 million tonnes per annum via its Lake Charles LNG project.

ET is self-funding its capex, and boosting its free cash flows. The company boasts 90% fee-based revenues and had its balance sheet upgraded to BBB by S&P and Fitch. ET’s leverage ratios have declined to the lower part of its 4.0 to 4.5x target range.

Insiders and independent board members own over 10% of the common units, with over 44 million units ($468 million) purchased since January 2021. The executive chairman, Mr. Kelcy Warren has notably purchased 61 million units ($675 million) since January 2019. The company’s co-CEOs hold 6x their annual base salary in ET units, overall indicating a strong alignment between management’s strategy and objectives and unit holder returns.

Pick #2: EIPI – Yield 7.3%

Perhaps the only notable drawback of investing in MLPs is the dreaded Schedule K-1, which tends to bring undesired complications in the tax filing process. While many see the benefits of tax-deferred distributions, some find the hassle to be unworthy of the pursuit. There are also international investors who experience highly unfavorable tax withholding by investing in Master Limited Partnerships.

FT Energy Income Partners Enhanced Income ETF (EIPI) is a diversified fund with 102 holdings and $962.3 million in net assets. EIPI is deeply diversified in the energy industry, with significant exposure to midstream leaders like EPD, ET, OneOK Inc. (OKE), MPLX, Kinder Morgan (KMI), Enbridge (ENB), etc. Source

About 90% of EIPI’s assets are invested in oil and gas and utility firms, providing solid exposure to not just MLPs but the broader fee- and infrastructure-heavy midstream and utility business, with relatively stable earnings despite economic volatility.

EIPI was formed in May 2024 through the merger and conversion of CEFs FEN and FEI. Since it began trading in its new structure as an ETF, EIPI has delivered solid total returns, beating that of the S&P 500, and the energy and utility indices.

This fund boosts returns by selling covered call options on securities it owns, earning extra income but capping some upside. The strategy balances dividends, call premiums, and growth potential to optimize returns. Notably, the average time until the expiration of calls stood at 38 days, indicating a relatively short-term options strategy. The fund sells Call-Out-Of-The-Money options with strike prices ~8.29% higher than current market prices, which makes them less likely to be exercised unless the equity prices rise rapidly (which is a bit rare in the boring midstream business). It is also important to note that ~38% of EIPI’s portfolio is tied to written covered calls, indicating over a third of the holdings are used to enhance returns through option premiums.

EIPI pays monthly distributions of $0.125/month, reflecting a 7.3% annualized yield, significantly exceeding the yield of its top two holdings, EPD and ET. Notably, the ETF paid a massive special payment in December to distribute short-term capital gains realized from its active portfolio management.

EIPI presents a solid choice to obtain diversified exposure to midstream energy firms, collect great monthly income, and avoid the pesky Schedule K-1!

Conclusion

Income-focused investors, particularly those looking toward retirement, must seek out dividends without drama. Midstream energy companies offer this compelling opportunity as these businesses are built to endure through various economic cycles and political climates, providing stable, predictable cash flow and a reliable dividend stream. By focusing on long-term, fee-based contracts and minimizing exposure to volatile commodity prices, midstream companies provide much-needed income stability in retirement. This type of investment is exactly what many retirees and income-seeking investors should aspire to, a secure, inflation-resistant cash flow that allows for peace of mind as you navigate the complexities of your financial future.

This article discusses two picks, Energy Transfer and EIPI, a midstream energy focused ETF. The former provides the benefits of deferred tax distributions, while the latter provides investors an option to benefit from this cash-flow-rich sector without the complexities of a Schedule K-1. Investing is all about making choices that align with your personal financial goals, and when it comes to income investing, there's always an option tailored to fit every investor's needs. In the ever-changing landscape, it's businesses like these that can help expand your income while providing the stability you need to meet your financial goals. This is the beauty of our Income Method, and the power of income investing.

data.title: BWX Technologies: A Strong Nuclear Bet, But With Little Margin For Error
data.description:
Investment Thesis

BWX Technologies, Inc. (BWXT) is a major player in the energy field, where federal projects drive about 80% of its income. That income is getting a boost from smart acquisitions—like Aerojet Ordnance Tennessee and Kinectrics—that give the company a stronger foothold in specialty materials and nuclear medicine. Those deals have put BWXT on a growth trajectory.

The company has secured some high-profile million-dollar defense contracts and just opened an innovation center in Virginia where it's developing fresh nuclear technologies for use on land, at sea and even in space.

While increased investments and changes to its project mix have dented profitability, BWXT still managed a 3% revenue increase by the end of 2024. It's optimistic about strong growth ahead—especially in its commercial division. But that growth could be hindered by a recent rise in debt.

Although BWXT remains profitable, it needs to get more efficient and turn more revenue into cash flow. Even with a strong business and promising growth prospects, I consider that BWXT's shares look expensive compared to both the industry and its own history. If those valuations are any guide, there's not much room for error.

That makes it a good idea for long-term investors who already own the stock to hold with it. For those considering getting in, though waiting for a market correction or a better entry point may be wise.

Overview

BWXT is a leading provider of nuclear components and fuel to the U.S. government and commercial sector. Its capabilities range from supplying nuclear propulsion for NASA projects to powering U.S. Navy submarines and aircraft carriers. It also provides energy to homes and businesses and supplies materials to medical facilities using nuclear technology.

Though the industry includes several branches, it generates 80% of its revenue from contracts with the U.S. government. And so, the defense sector's projections for the years ahead are critical to consider. As I shared in my analysis of Woodward, Inc. (WWD):

"The U.S. aerospace and defense market is projected to grow at an annual rate of 2.4% until 2028, focused on investments in defense and upward demand for both commercial and military aerospace products."

In its plan to stay at the front of new ideas, BWXT has done big buys and deals in the first months of 2025. On January 3, it finished the acquisition of Aerojet Ordnance Tennessee, Inc. (A.O.T), strengthening its special materials work in the government area with key items like used uranium and tungsten. ͏ Days later, on January 7, it announced it was buying Kinectrics for $525 million, growing its role in the nuclear and medicine isotope areas through reactor life services and medical isotope making. Then, on January 27, BWXT got deals worth more than C$1 billion to make the Pickering nuclear plant last longer and build a small modular reactor in Darlington, Ontario, boosting the Canadian nuclear field.

Also, on February 19, it announced the concession of $2.1 billion in deals to make naval nuclear reactor parts for submarines and aircraft carriers, with work going until 2030. To match its growth into new technologies, on March 26, it opened its Innovation Campus in Lynchburg, Virginia, dedicated to the work of nuclear tech for land, sea, and space uses. With more than 350 workers on projects for NASA, the Department of Energy and the Department of Defense and a $65 million investment it pushes big projects like Project Pele mobile microreactor and the making of nuclear thermal drive for space travel which puts its position as a main player in energy safety and tech innovation

4Q 2024

According to company data, Q4 2024 ended with a 3% increase in revenue (from $726 MM to $746 MM), driven by the very good performance of Commercial Operations, although partially offset by a slight decline in the Government Operations segment.

However, the adjustment in the project mix and the higher level of investments had a negative effect on profitability: adjusted EBITDA was $130 MM, 12% below Q4 2023. Non-GAAP EPS also contracted by 9%.

Within Government Operations, revenues reached $595 MM, 1% below the previous year, given the lower purchase of long-term materials and a decrease in the processing of special materials, despite the increase in the manufacturing of naval components and microreactor activities.

Meanwhile, Commercial Operations had 23% growth in revenues, and revenues reached $152 MM in Q4 2024, driven by strong demand in the medical segment and a higher volume of commercial nuclear components and fuels.

Outlook

According to BWXT's estimates, it expects solid growth in both segments, with Government Operations increasing at a mid-single-digit pace, driven by its activities and the recent acquisition of A.O.T., while for Commercial Operations they project an increase of around 50%, with a double-digit increase, adding the contribution of Kinectrics

At the level of profitability, the company projects adjusted EBITDA margins of approximately 20% in Government Operations and between 14% and 15% in Commercial Operations.

On the other hand, despite the increase in debt, which will increase interest expense between $10 MM and $12 MM, it expects to maintain solid free cash flow, with a capex rate of between 5% and 6% of sales.

In terms of net profitability, it foresees a slight increase in the tax rate due to the change in the geographical composition of income, while planned share buybacks will offset the dilution, keeping the number of shares outstanding stable.

Valuation

Analyzing aspects such as growth, profitability and efficiency, I created a combining profile for the stock. Nonetheless, the profile in its current state poses limitations to the firm’s long-term attractiveness.

I will begin with the company’s profitability. It is noteworthy to mention that the company holds a couple of strongest segments: its net margin is 10.4%, higher than the industry’s 6.2%, meaning the company’s profit conversion from income is more efficient than others.

Likewise, given that ROE is 28 percent, with BWXT outperforming the company’s 12.7 percent industry, it indicates that the company is wisely using capital from investors– clearly BWXT is effectively employing investment funds. However, assessing ROTC and ROA data seems to indicate that while the business is profitable, its operating efficiency relative to the average over the last five years is concerning.

Moreover, with EPS estimating a growth from 5.3% to 30.7% between 2025 and 2028, the results indicate a promising outlook. Although, in my assessment, the market has already integrated the expectations into the property’s value based on multi criteria analysis with other indicators.

Here’s where I see some red flags about BWXT, given that the multiples seem too high both in the company’s history as well as against the sector. The P/E [FWD] is 28.9x, higher than the sector’s 19.1x which suggests a premium of 45.1%. Other indicators which are not favorable for valuation include the EV/EBITDA ratio of 18.3x versus 10.5x for the sector, EV/EBIT of 22.5x compared to 15.1x for the sector, and P/B of 7.05x against 2.9x in the sector. It is my opinion that these ratios do not allow much margin for strategic execution success from the company.

In the same fashion, the P/S is elevated both in TTM and FWD at 3.4x and 3.05x respectively compared to the sector’s 1.4x and 1.36x. From what has been said, indicates that there is a proportionate decline in return for investment on revenue generation. To further validate the point, the PEG GAAP and Non-GAAP (2.25x and 2.43x) clearly show the stock is not cheap.

In analyzing the cash flow, I attempted to gain a medium to long-term perspective on how the entity has the potential to finance growth. Although it is better than the last five-year average by 33.2%, its free cash flow margin of 4.1% is lower than the industry average of 6.8%. This may signal that the company might face some challenges in meeting its growth targets without incurring additional debt or equity financing.

Risks

The industry in which the company operates is highly regulated. So, changes in the regulations could restrict business expansion due to increased costs. It is also exposed to penalties for failing to comply, not to mention the usual damage to reputation.

While there is a global change towards cleaner sources of energy, and a favorable attitude to nuclear energy, it has to be recognized that there are several other competitors in the renewable energy segment. The term “nuclear” usually raises some safety alarms. The perception along with heightened competition in the use of an alternative energy source could have unfavorable impacts on demand.

Continuing with competition issues, there is strong growth in investment in the sector from China and Russia, which could reduce the US market share, and therefore, that of BWXT. One of the main challenges I see is in the expansion into the civilian market, since the company must compete with already established firms.

Another point I consider important is the company's strong dependence on government contracts. According to the figures for the last two years, these represented 80% of the company's income. This may be considered a strength since it provides a source of income stability it also opens the business to potential defense contract cancellations or cutbacks.

Finally, BWXT has a very high valuation, above its industry. Moreover, in an industry where ongoing innovation is vital, its cash flow is weak and its profit margin is narrow.

Conclusion

Based on what has been analyzed, I conclude that BWXT is a company with solid fundamentals in terms of profitability and growth prospects. I consider that its current valuation is high compared to its history and the sector. While EPS projections are optimistic, the market already priced in accelerated growth, leaving little room for negative surprises.

Therefore, for the long term, the company could justify its valuation if it managed to expand its margins and improve its conversion of revenue to cash flow. However, in my view, for investors with a focus on value, the stock looks expensive at the current price. It is necessary to monitor the performance of the coming months in terms of price.

For those long-term investors who have the asset in their portfolio, I recommend to hold it, while for those who wish to enter, I estimate that a more attractive entry point could occur in a market correction or in a reduction of its valuation multiples to levels more aligned with its historical average.
